title: Svíčková na Smetaně
subtitle: Czech braised beef sirloin with cream sauce
excerpt: Beef sirloin slowly braised on a bed of root vegetables, then sliced and served under a silky, sweet-sour cream sauce with a slice of bread dumpling and a spoon of cranberry. The Czech national dish.
sourceNotes: Svíčková na smetaně is Czechia's most iconic dish, served on Sundays and at celebrations. Documented in Lesley Chamberlain, The Food and Cooking of Eastern Europe (1989) and Josephine Bacon, The Complete Book of Czech and Slovak Cooking (1999). The sweet-sour cream sauce (made from the braising vegetables) is unique to Czech cooking.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: The braised beef and sauce can be made a day ahead. Reheat the sliced beef in the sauce gently.

content.text: Svíčková na smetaně is not a dish you make quickly. The beef needs 2 to 2.5 hours in the oven; the sauce needs patience and the right balance of sweet and sour to be correct. But the result is tender sliced beef under a pale, velvety cream sauce that is simultaneously sweet, sharp, and deeply savoury — unlike anything else.

The sauce is made by blending the braising vegetables (carrot, parsnip, celeriac, onion) with the cooking liquid and cream. Its character comes from lemon juice or vinegar to sharpen it, sugar to balance, and a good amount of cream poured in at the end. Cranberry jam (brusinky) is the traditional accompaniment, spooned on top of the sauce at the table.

items.prepNote: juice only, plus a little zest

content.text: Heat the oven to 160°C fan. Melt the butter in a large casserole over high heat and sear the beef joint on all sides until deep brown, about 8 minutes. Transfer to a plate. Add all the vegetables, lemon juice and zest, bay, allspice, peppercorns, and thyme to the casserole. Stir for 2 minutes. Nestle the beef on top. Add the wine and stock — the liquid should come about halfway up the joint. Cover tightly and braise for 2 to 2.5 hours until very tender.

Remove the beef and rest it. Strain the braising liquid and reserve. Put the cooked vegetables into a blender with enough braising liquid to blend smoothly. Pour into a saucepan and add the cream, vinegar, sugar, and salt. Simmer for 5 minutes, stirring. Adjust the sweet-sour balance: it should be noticeably sweet-sharp, not neutral. Slice the beef and reheat briefly in the sauce or return it to the pot and ladle the sauce over.

items.cause: Vegetables not blended finely enough, or too much braising liquid added
items.fix: Simmer uncovered to reduce; or add a tablespoon of plain flour stirred into a little cold cream before adding to the pan

items.symptom: Sauce tastes flat — missing the sweet-sour balance
items.fix: Add both in small increments and taste; the sweet and sour should be in balance, both present and neither dominant

content.text: Svíčková na smetaně is Sunday lunch in Czechia. It arrives with two slices of bread dumpling (houskový knedlík) placed beside the beef and a generous spoon of cranberry compote (brusinky) on top of the sauce — the cranberry is not garnish but part of the flavour. Czechs have strong opinions about the correct sweet-sour ratio in the sauce, and every family has its own calibrated version.
